title: The Choosing of the Twelve
subtitle: Christ keeps watch all night upon the mountain, and at dawn names His Apostles

stillness: Rise up, you also, in the storm of the night.
Purer at that hour is the soul; the very darkness and the great silence are enough to lead you to compunction.
Enter into your inner room, and with the door shut, pray.
gospel: It came to pass in those days,
that He went out into a mountain to pray,
and He passed the whole night in the prayer of God.
He called unto Him His disciples,
and He chose twelve of them,
behold: Behold each thing as if you were present.
See the Lord withdraw from the crowds, away from their noise, and go up alone into the mountain to pray.
He who prays well lifts the mind upward, away from earthly things; and for this a stillness of the body is needful.
He keeps watch all night in the prayer of God, not of the world, not for Himself as one weak and in need, but for us, as one tender and merciful.
He prays alone. Nowhere, if I am not mistaken, is He found to have prayed together with the Apostles.
Everywhere He entreats alone: the counsel of God human prayers cannot contain.
Not only hiding Himself from the crowds, but admitting not one of the disciples, not one of His own.
A pattern is given you; a form is set down for you to imitate.
For what ought you to do for your own salvation, when Christ keeps watch the whole night in prayer for you?
consider: Consider this: that Christ, about to choose the Apostles, prayed at length, for He was passing the whole night in prayer.
And so, with long and nightly prayer set first, He chose the Apostles.
By this He shows that devout prayers must go before the raising of prelates in the Church of God.
And that they are to be chosen through devout prayers, not through pressure and promises.
It was not of their own choosing or striving, but of the divine favour and grace, that they were called to the Apostolate.
Not you have chosen me, but I have chosen you.
So the Lord shuts out human striving from the choice of a prelate: human petitions, money, gain, lineage, and the like.
That in it grace alone may run its course, by which and through which alone a man ought to be chosen.
He set the Apostles upon the mountain, signifying that bishops, the successors of the Apostles, stand in the higher rank of the Church.
They are called overseers, who ought to watch over the Lord's flock, that they may lift it up to the things above.
But, alas! many today are lower in their works than their flock; they profit neither themselves nor others, and so they hold the name without the thing.
When Christ prays, He shapes the contemplative life; when He teaches, the active.
That no one, for care of his neighbour, may grow numb in the pursuit of contemplation;
nor, for the pursuit of divine contemplation, however higher it be, may neglect the care of his neighbour.
The nocturnal tear surpasses every dew; it avails against concupiscence and against every swelling of pride.
But if a man is not warmed by that dew, he withers under the day.
prayer: who out of your inestimable mercy came into the world,
to call sinners back from their wanderings to repentance,
and of these were pleased to choose many to be your confidants and your own disciples:
recall me, O merciful God, a wretched sinner astray;
and at the last, admitting me, though unworthy, among your disciples,
lift me wholly from the desire of earthly things,
and carry my mind altogether to the things of heaven,
that I may hear and understand your words, and fulfil them all in deed. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Et erat pernoctans in oratione Dei, non seculi; non pro se tamquam infirmus et indigens, sed pro nobis tamquam pius et clemens.
latinJewel.english: And He kept watch all night long in the prayer of God, not of the world; not for Himself as one weak and in need, but for us, as one tender and merciful.
